title: About
text:
I’m Adam Ezzat — a Data Scientist and Data Analyst based in Cupertino, CA, with a background that spans economics, business analytics, and deep learning. I build machine learning models, design data pipelines, and — in my own time — build programming languages from scratch.
My professional work focuses on forecasting, statistical modeling, and turning messy real-world data into systems that actually make decisions better. My personal work is where I push further: CJC-Lang, a deterministic scientific computing language written entirely in Rust, is the project where everything I’ve learned converges.

title: About
text:
The CJC-Lang Project
Outside of my professional work, I’m building CJC-Lang (Computational Jacobian Core) — a deterministic scientific computing language implemented as a 21-crate Rust workspace. It’s where my data science background meets systems programming: a language with built-in tensors, automatic differentiation, statistical distributions, and guaranteed reproducibility. The compiler, runtime, and all core libraries are built from scratch with nearly zero external dependencies.
CJC-Lang is in active beta — the full compiler pipeline, dual execution engines, ~300 builtins, and end-to-end ML benchmarks are all working. It’s a serious technical project, not a toy, but it’s not yet production-ready.
You can read more about it in the blog post or on the projects page.

title: Chess RL in CJC-Lang: Training a Chess Agent from Scratch with Zero Dependencies
text:
🕑 ~20 min read
I built a chess-playing AI from scratch — in a programming language I also built from scratch. No PyTorch. No NumPy. No Stockfish. Zero external dependencies.
This post is a technical walkthrough of how that works: the language design decisions that made it possible, the RL algorithm, the chess engine, the autodiff engine, the performance engineering, and what I learned along the way.

title: Chess RL in CJC-Lang: Training a Chess Agent from Scratch with Zero Dependencies
text:
What is CJC-Lang?
CJC-Lang (Computational Jacobian Core) is a numerical programming language I wrote in Rust — 21 crates, roughly 96,000 lines of code. It’s designed for ML pipelines, numerical systems, and scientific computing where reproducibility matters.
Reproducibility is a first-class concern, not an afterthought. The language enforces it through specific engineering choices:

Kahan summation for all floating-point reductions — prevents accumulation error from corrupting results
Ordered data structures throughout (BTreeMap/BTreeSet, never HashMap) — no iteration-order non-determinism
Explicit seed threading via SplitMix64 — every source of randomness is traceable
No FMA instructions in SIMD paths — fused multiply-add can produce different results on different hardware
Two execution backends (AST interpreter + MIR register machine) that are required to produce identical results

The result: same input, same output. Every time.
CJC-Lang has built-in tensors, automatic differentiation, copy-on-write memory, pattern matching, closures, structs, for/while loops, if-expressions, file I/O, and SVG visualization. The compiler pipeline looks like this:




flowchart LR
    S["Source\n.cjcl"] --> L["Lexer"] --> P["Parser"] --> A["AST"] --> T["Type\nChecker"] --> H["HIR"] --> M["MIR"] --> E["Executor"]


CJC-Lang compiler pipeline. Source code flows through seven stages before execution.



The chess RL demo exercises nearly every one of these language features — it’s the most demanding program CJC-Lang runs.

title: Chess RL in CJC-Lang: Training a Chess Agent from Scratch with Zero Dependencies
Chess RL in CJC-Lang: Training a Chess Agent from Scratch with Zero Dependencies
Chess RL in CJC-Lang: Training a Chess Agent from Scratch with Zero Dependencies
text:
The Autodiff Engine
This is where CJC-Lang’s runtime does the heavy lifting. The RL algorithm above calls grad_graph.backward(loss) — but what happens inside that call?

GradGraph: Arena-Based Automatic Differentiation
CJC-Lang’s autodiff engine is a reverse-mode automatic differentiation system. It builds a directed acyclic graph (DAG) of operations during the forward pass, then traverses it backward to compute gradients.
The original implementation (v2.0) used the standard Rust approach: Vec<Rc<RefCell<GradNode>>> — each node was a reference-counted, interior-mutable object. This works, but every .borrow() call has runtime overhead, and each node allocation hits the heap individually.
In v2.4, the engine was redesigned to use arena allocation: flat arrays for operations, tensors, and gradients. This eliminated roughly 120 .borrow() calls per backward pass.
v2.0: Each node is a separate heap allocation
  [Node 0] → [Node 1] → [Node 2] → ...  (Rc<RefCell<GradNode>> per node)

v2.4+: Flat arena arrays — one contiguous block
  ops:     [Op0, Op1, Op2, Op3, ...]      (Vec<GradOp>)
  tensors: [T0,  T1,  T2,  T3,  ...]      (Vec<Tensor>)
  grads:   [G0,  G1,  G2,  G3,  ...]      (Vec<Option<Tensor>>)


Dead Node Elimination
Not every node in the computation graph is reachable from the loss. In a multi-head architecture (policy + value), the backward pass from the policy loss doesn’t need gradients for nodes only used by the value head (and vice versa during the value backward pass).
Before traversing backward, the engine builds a reachability set from the loss node. Unreachable nodes are skipped entirely — typically 20-30% of the graph for multi-head networks.


In-Place Gradient Accumulation (v2.5)
The original gradient accumulation created a new tensor every time two gradients were added:
// Before: allocates a new tensor
grads[idx] = Some(existing.add_unchecked(&incoming));
In v2.5, this was replaced with in-place mutation:
// After: mutates existing tensor
existing.add_assign_unchecked(&incoming);
This uses Buffer::borrow_data_mut() to get a mutable reference to the underlying data and update it element-wise. The result: roughly half the tensor allocations per backward pass are eliminated. This single change was responsible for the v2.4 → v2.5 speedup (43.5 → 29.8 seconds per episode).


Fused MLP Layers (v2.5)
A standard dense layer in the computation graph creates four separate nodes:

Transpose the weight matrix
Matrix multiply input by transposed weights
Add bias
Apply activation function

The fused GradOp::MlpLayer collapses all four into a single graph node:
MlpLayer {
    input: usize,    // index of input tensor
    weight: usize,   // index of weight tensor
    bias: usize,     // index of bias tensor
    activation: Activation,  // ReLU, Tanh, etc.
}
This reduces graph nodes by 3x per layer and computes all three parameter gradients (d_input, d_weight, d_bias) in a single fused backward pass.

title: Chess RL in CJC-Lang: Training a Chess Agent from Scratch with Zero Dependencies
text:
Performance Engineering

The Profiling-Driven Approach
Every optimization was driven by measurement, not intuition. CJC-Lang has built-in profiling builtins (profile_zone_start, profile_zone_stop, profile_dump) that I used to instrument the training loop.
The first profile revealed the bottleneck distribution:



Function
% of Rollout Time




score_moves (forward pass per move)
84.2%


legal_moves (move generation)
7.7%


apply_move (board update)
0.1%


Everything else
8.0%



84% of time was spent evaluating the neural network for each legal move. The fix: score_moves_batch, a native Rust builtin that evaluates all legal moves in a single batched forward pass instead of looping through them one at a time in the interpreter.
Result: 7.7x rollout speedup.


The Amdahl’s Law Moment
Here’s where it got interesting. A 7.7x speedup on the rollout phase should have been transformative. But total episode time barely moved:
Before: rollout ~10.4s + backward ~25s = ~35s
After:  rollout ~1.3s  + backward ~25s = ~26s
Speedup: ~1.35x (not 7.7x)
Amdahl’s Law in action. The rollout was only about 30% of total episode time. Making it 7.7x faster saved ~9 seconds, but the backward pass (which was untouched) still took 25 seconds. The bottleneck had shifted.
This insight redirected the entire optimization strategy. Instead of making the interpreter faster, I redesigned the autodiff engine:



Version
Change
Episode Time
Speedup




v2.3
Native rollout kernels
82.2 s
baseline


v2.4
Arena GradGraph + dead node elimination
43.5 s
1.89x


v2.5
In-place gradients + fused MLP
29.8 s
2.76x





Native Hot-Path Kernels
Three performance-critical functions were promoted from CJC-Lang code to native Rust builtins:
adam_step — Element-wise Adam optimizer update. The CJC-Lang version was a scalar loop over every parameter; the native version operates directly on tensor buffers. ~9x faster.
encode_state_fast — Board-to-tensor encoding. The CJC-Lang version used array_push to build the 774-element feature vector, which triggered a copy-on-write clone at each push. The native version fills a pre-allocated buffer in a single pass. O(n) vs O(38n).
score_moves_batch — Batched move scoring. Instead of calling the forward pass once per legal move (in the interpreter), this evaluates all moves in a single native call with one matrix multiplication. 7.7x rollout speedup.
All three builtins produce bit-identical output to their CJC-Lang counterparts — verified by parity tests.

title: Chess RL in CJC-Lang: Training a Chess Agent from Scratch with Zero Dependencies
text:
Training Results

Honest Numbers
After 120 episodes of self-play training:

Elo change: +13.9 — the first measurable learning signal in the v2.x series
Win rate vs random: 50% — not yet reliably beating a random opponent
Win rate vs greedy: 50% — ties with a material-greedy baseline
Non-zero terminal rewards: 6/120 episodes — most games are still drawn

These are modest results. The agent is not strong. I report this honestly because the goal was never to compete with Stockfish or AlphaZero — it was to demonstrate that a from-scratch language can express, train, and evaluate a real RL agent.

The Stack

    Chess engine + A2C + GAE + Adam + Elo + PGN + checkpoints

CJC-Lang compiler + runtime (Rust, ~96K LOC)
    Lexer → Parser → AST → Type Checker → HIR → MIR → Executor

Every tensor operation, every gradient computation, every optimizer step runs in code I own. No framework sits between the algorithm and the math.
Same seed = same game. Always.

title: Chess RL in CJC-Lang: Training a Chess Agent from Scratch with Zero Dependencies
text:
What’s Next
The agent’s Elo +13.9 is a starting point, not a destination. The infrastructure is now solid — the bottleneck is no longer the language or the autodiff engine, it’s the ML itself. Larger networks, longer training, curriculum learning, and MCTS are all expressible in CJC-Lang as it stands today.
The deeper goal isn’t chess strength. It’s showing that a carefully designed language — one that takes reproducibility seriously, owns its entire stack, and grows to meet the demands of its hardest workload — can do real work.
If you want to try CJC-Lang yourself: cargo install cjc-lang, then cjcl run your_program.cjcl.

CJC-Lang is open source. The compiler, runtime, and all demos are available on GitHub.

title: CJC-Lang v0.1.0: The First Public Release
text:
It Ships
Today I published CJC-Lang v0.1.0 — the first public release of a programming language I’ve been building from scratch in Rust. Twenty crates, roughly 96,000 lines of code, zero external runtime dependencies.
cargo install cjc-lang
That’s it. One command, one binary, everything included.
If you read my earlier post introducing CJC-Lang, you know the motivation: I wanted a language where numerical computation is deterministic by default. Not “mostly reproducible” or “reproducible if you pin every dependency.” Deterministic. Same seed in, same bits out, every time, on every platform.
v0.1.0 is the first version I’m confident enough to put a number on.

title: CJC-Lang v0.1.0: The First Public Release
CJC-Lang v0.1.0: The First Public Release
text:
What’s Not in v0.1.0
I want to be upfront about the limitations:

Single-file only: The module system (mod, import) is parsed but not functional. Every program lives in one .cjcl file.
No package manager: There’s a design (ADR-0013) for a git-based deterministic package manager, but it’s not implemented.
No formatter: No cjcl fmt yet.
Parser recovery is limited: A syntax error mid-function loses the rest of that function body. You still get the error with line/column, but you only get one error per function.
Visibility not enforced: pub and priv are parsed but everything is treated as public.
Some CLI commands are stubs: mem and trace are placeholders.

These are all solvable. None of them compromise the core promise: deterministic, reproducible numerical computing.

text: 🕑 ~45 min read

title: Syntax Guide for CJC Version 0.1.1
Syntax Guide for CJC Version 0.1.1
text:
Part 2 — The Compiler Pipeline
CJC compiles through a multi-stage pipeline. Understanding it helps you reason about error messages, optimization, and the dual-engine execution model.
Source → Lexer → Parser → AST → [TypeChecker] → HIR → MIR → [Optimize] → Exec

Stage 1: Lexer
The lexer (cjc-lexer) converts source text into a flat token stream. It recognizes all CJC-Lang keywords, operators (including |>, +=, @), literals (integers, floats, strings, byte strings), and identifiers.
let (tokens, diags) = Lexer::new(src).tokenize();


Stage 2: Parser
A Pratt parser (cjc-parser) builds the AST from tokens. It handles operator precedence, expression vs. statement disambiguation, and produces structured error diagnostics.
let (program, diags) = cjc_parser::parse_source(src);
The parser recognizes 35 expression kinds, 9 statement kinds, 11 declaration kinds, and 9 pattern kinds.


Stage 3: AST
The AST (cjc-ast) is a tree of typed nodes. As of v0.1.1, the AST layer includes:

Visitor trait (visit module) — read-only traversal covering all node variants with walk_* functions
Metrics (metrics module) — single-pass structural statistics: node counts, depth, operator frequencies, feature flags
Validation (validate module) — structural checks: break/continue outside loops, duplicate params, empty match, unreachable code after return, nesting depth limits
Inspect (inspect module) — deterministic text dumps: dump_ast_summary, dump_ast_metrics, dump_validation_report, dump_expr_tree
Node utilities (node_utils module) — helper methods like Expr::child_count, is_literal, Block::is_empty, Program::has_main_function



Stage 4: HIR (High-level IR)
HIR lowering (cjc-hir) desugars the AST. for loops become while loops over ranges. Closures get capture analysis. The HIR is closer to the execution model while still being readable.


Stage 5: MIR (Mid-level IR)
MIR (cjc-mir) is a register-based intermediate representation. HIR lowers to MIR instructions that operate on virtual registers.
The MIR layer in v0.1.1 includes:

Optimizer — constant folding, strength reduction, dead code elimination (DCE), common subexpression elimination (CSE), loop-invariant code motion (LICM)
SSA construction — Cytron’s minimal SSA algorithm with 6 optimization passes
Loop analysis — enriched LoopInfo with is_countable, trip_count_hint, num_exits, schedule metadata
Reduction analysis — enriched ReductionInfo with accumulator_semantics (Plain, Kahan, Binned, RuntimeDefined)
Schedule metadata — descriptive execution hints (SequentialStrict, DescriptiveTiled, DescriptiveVectorized, etc.) that are non-semantic
SSA loop overlay — maps SSA definitions to loops, identifies loop-carried variables, cross-references reductions with SSA accumulators
Legality verifier — schedule consistency checks, reduction metadata cross-validation
NoGC verifier — call-graph may_gc fixpoint analysis



Stage 6: Execution (Dual Engine)
CJC-Lang has two execution engines that must produce identical results:









Engine
Module
Entry Point
Description




v1
cjc-eval
Interpreter::new(seed).exec(&program)
AST tree-walk interpreter


v2
cjc-mir-exec
run_program_with_executor(&program, seed)
MIR register-machine executor



The MIR executor also supports an optimized mode:
cjcl run program.cjcl --mir-opt
Parity tests in the test suite verify that both engines produce bit-identical output for every supported program.


Diagnostics
CJC-Lang uses structured error codes (E0xxx-E8xxx) via the cjc-diag crate. Diagnostics carry source spans, severity levels, and optional fix suggestions.

title: Syntax Guide for CJC Version 0.1.1
text:
Part 3 — Determinism Architecture
Determinism is CJC-Lang’s defining architectural constraint. Every design decision flows from one requirement: same seed in, same bits out, every time, on every platform.

How CJC Achieves Determinism







Component
Mechanism




RNG
SplitMix64 with explicit seed threading — no global state


Collections
BTreeMap/BTreeSet everywhere — never HashMap/HashSet


Floating-point sums
Kahan summation or BinnedAccumulator — never naive reduction


SIMD
No FMA (fused multiply-add) — mul_fixed throughout


Complex arithmetic
mul_fixed for all complex multiplications


Iteration
Vec-based storage with deterministic ordering


Parallelism
Results identical regardless of thread count





Kahan Summation
Naive floating-point addition loses precision as terms accumulate. CJC-Lang uses compensated summation everywhere:
// Under the hood, CJC-Lang's runtime uses Kahan summation:
// sum = 0.0, compensation = 0.0
// for each term:
//   y = term - compensation
//   t = sum + y
//   compensation = (t - sum) - y
//   sum = t
This matters for scientific workloads where you’re summing millions of terms. The result is the same whether you sum them on a laptop or a cluster.


BinnedAccumulator
For even higher-precision requirements, CJC-Lang provides binned accumulators that partition the floating-point range into bins, accumulate within each bin, then combine — guaranteeing reproducible results even under reordering.


SplitMix64
All randomness in CJC-Lang flows through a seeded SplitMix64 generator. There is no global RNG state. Every function that needs randomness takes an explicit seed parameter:
let samples = q_sample(circuit, 1000, 42);
let measurement = q_measure(circuit, 42);

array_get(arr, idx)
Get element at index


array_set(arr, idx, val)
Set element (returns new array)


array_slice(arr, start, end)
Subsequence


array_contains(arr, val)
Membership test


array_reverse(arr)
Reverse


array_sort(arr)
Sort (deterministic)


array_map(arr, f)
Map function over elements


array_filter(arr, f)
Filter by predicate


array_reduce(arr, f, init)
Left fold


array_flatten(arr)
Flatten nested arrays


array_zip(a, b)
Zip two arrays


array_unique(arr)
Remove duplicates (deterministic)





Strings



Function
Description




str_len(s)
Length


str_upper(s), str_lower(s)
Case conversion


str_trim(s)
Strip whitespace


str_split(s, delim)
Split by delimiter


str_join(arr, delim)
Join array with delimiter


str_contains(s, sub)
Substring test


str_replace(s, old, new)
Replace occurrences


str_starts_with(s, prefix)
Prefix test


str_ends_with(s, suffix)
Suffix test


str_char_at(s, idx)
Character at index


str_substring(s, start, end)
Substring extraction





Tensor Operations







Function
Description




Tensor.from_vec(data, shape)
Create tensor from flat data + shape


t.shape()
Get shape


t.sum()
Sum all elements


t.mean()
Mean of all elements


t.transpose()
Transpose


t.matmul(other)
Matrix multiplication


t.layer_norm(gamma, beta)
Layer normalization


t.linear(weights, bias)
Linear transform


t.split_heads(n)
Split into attention heads


t.merge_heads()
Merge attention heads


t.softmax()
Softmax activation


t.relu()
ReLU activation



Tensor operations use SIMD acceleration internally but never use FMA, preserving bit-identical results.


I/O



Function
Description




print(val)
Print to stdout


read_file(path)
Read file contents


write_file(path, content)
Write file


to_string(val)
Convert to string representation





Automatic Differentiation
CJC-Lang has built-in AD support via cjc-ad:



Function
Description




dual(value, derivative)
Create forward-mode dual number


grad(f, x)
Compute gradient


jacobian(f, x)
Compute Jacobian matrix



Forward-mode uses dual numbers; reverse-mode uses a deterministic tape with gradient graphs.

title: Syntax Guide for CJC Version 0.1.1
text:
Part 5 — DataFrame Operations
CJC-Lang includes a tidyverse-inspired DataFrame library (cjc-data) for tabular data manipulation:
let df = dataframe([
    column("name", ["Alice", "Bob", "Charlie"]),
    column("age", [30, 25, 35]),
    column("score", [95.5, 87.3, 91.2]),
]);

// Filter, transform, aggregate
df |> filter(col("age") > 25)
   |> mutate("grade", col("score") / 10.0)
   |> group_by(col("grade"))
   |> summarize("avg_age", mean(col("age")))
Key operations:



Operation
Description




filter(predicate)
Row selection


select(columns...)
Column selection


mutate(name, expr)
Add/transform column


group_by(column)
Group rows


summarize(name, agg)
Aggregate within groups


join(other, on)
Join two DataFrames


arrange(column)
Sort by column

text:
Part 9 — CLI Reference
CJC-Lang ships as a single binary with 29 subcommands across three tiers.

File Format Compatibility
Not every command operates on .cjcl source files. Here’s what each command reads:
CJC-Lang source only (.cjcl): run, proof, emit, explain, gc, nogc, audit, precision, parity, test, bench, mem, trace, lock, check
CJC + other formats (read-only):







Command
Formats




doctor
.cjcl, .snap (binary), .csv/.tsv, plus metadata on all files for size warnings


ci
.cjcl only (reimplements parse + type checks, does not run full doctor)


view
Directory metadata, .cjcl source for effect annotations, .snap binary for hashes


inspect
Dispatches on extension: .snap (binary decode), .csv/.tsv (schema), .cjcl (source), anything else (generic: size + hash)


seek
Traverses any directory; reads any file’s content for --contains



Non-CJC-Lang formats exclusively:







Command
Formats




flow
.csv/.tsv or stdin


schema
.csv/.tsv


patch
.csv/.tsv or stdin


drift
Any two files (auto-detects .csv/.tsv for structured diff, otherwise text diff)





Core Commands







Command
Description




cjcl run file.cjcl
Execute a program (AST tree-walk interpreter)


cjcl run file.cjcl --mir-opt
Execute via MIR engine with optimizations (CF + DCE)


cjcl run file.cjcl --mir-mono
Execute with MIR monomorphization


cjcl lex file.cjcl
Show token stream


cjcl parse file.cjcl
Parse and pretty-print AST


cjcl check file.cjcl
Type-check without executing


cjcl repl
Interactive REPL (supports :ast, :mir, :type, :env, :seed meta-commands)





Global Flags (for core commands)



Flag
Description




--seed N
Set RNG seed (default: 42)


--reproducible
Enable reproducibility mode


--time
Print execution time


--mir-opt
Enable MIR optimizations


--mir-mono
Enable MIR monomorphization


--multi-file
Enable multi-file module resolution


--color / --no-color
Force color output on/off


--diagnostic-format rich\|short
Diagnostic output format





Phase 1 CLI Suite — Data & Pipeline Tools

cjcl view [path] — Deterministic directory listing
Lists directory contents with file kinds, sizes, and effect annotations. Output is sorted deterministically. Hash display is off by default for cleaner output; opt in with --hash when you need content-addressable verification.
Flags: -r, --recursive | --hash | --no-effects | --no-size | --no-header | --plain / --json / --table / --color
$ cjcl view examples/

  examples/ (17 entries)

┌───────────────────────────────────────────┬──────┬───────────┬─────────┐
│ Name                                      │ Kind │ Size      │ Effects │
├───────────────────────────────────────────┼──────┼───────────┼─────────┤
│ examples/chess_replay.html                │ file │ 17.9 KiB  │ -       │
│ examples/chess_rl_dashboard.html          │ file │ 11.4 KiB  │ -       │
│ examples/etl_csv_parse.cjcl               │ cjcl │ 774 B     │ -       │
│ examples/nlp_tokenize.cjcl                │ cjcl │ 766 B     │ -       │
│ examples/transformer_forward.cjcl         │ cjcl │ 5.7 KiB   │ -       │
│ examples/vizor_bar.cjcl                   │ cjcl │ 439 B     │ -       │
│ ...                                       │      │           │         │
└───────────────────────────────────────────┴──────┴───────────┴─────────┘


cjcl proof <file.cjcl> — Determinism & reproducibility profiler
Runs a program multiple times and verifies that stdout, exit status, and GC behavior are identical across runs. Supports testing across multiple seeds to catch seed-dependent nondeterminism.
Flags: -n, --runs <N> | --seed <N> | --seeds <N,M,...> | -v, --verbose | --plain / --json / --color
$ cjcl proof fibonacci.cjcl

[proof] Running 3 iterations with seed 42...
55
55
55

┌──────────────────────────┬────────┐
│ Check                    │ Status │
├──────────────────────────┼────────┤
│ stdout identical         │ PASS   │
│ exit status identical    │ PASS   │
│ GC collections identical │ PASS   │
└──────────────────────────┴────────┘

Verdict: PASS (3 runs, seed=42)
Multi-seed mode tests reproducibility across different RNG seeds in a single invocation:
$ cjcl proof fibonacci.cjcl --seeds 42,99,256


cjcl flow [file.csv] — Streaming computation engine
Applies aggregation operations to CSV columns using O(1) memory per column. Uses Kahan summation for numerically stable sums and Welford’s algorithm for online variance. Reads from stdin if no file is given.
Flags: -d, --delimiter <c> | --tsv | --no-header | --op <ops> (sum, mean, min, max, count, var, std) | -c, --columns <cols> | --precision <N> | --plain / --json / --color
$ cjcl flow data.csv --op sum

┌────────┬───────────┐
│ Column │ sum       │
├────────┼───────────┤
│ a      │ 12.000000 │
│ b      │ 15.000000 │
│ c      │ 18.000000 │
└────────┴───────────┘
(3 rows processed)


cjcl patch <file.csv> — Type-aware data transformation
Apply transforms like rename, drop, impute, and replace to CSV data.
Transforms: --nan-fill <value> | --impute <column> | --replace <col> <from> <to> | --drop <column> | --rename <old> <new> | --fill-empty <col> <value>
Flags: -d, --delimiter <c> | --tsv | --no-header | -o, --output <file> | --plain / --json / --color
$ cjcl patch data.csv --rename a x

x,b,c
1,2,3
4,5,6
7,8,9
patched 3 rows


cjcl seek [path] [pattern] — Deterministic file discovery
Find files by type, content, or size with deterministic ordering.
Flags: -t, --type <ext> | --contains <text> | --min-size <size> | --max-size <size> | --max-depth <N> | --count | --plain / --json / --color
$ cjcl seek examples/ --type cjc

examples/etl_csv_parse.cjcl
examples/nlp_tokenize.cjcl
examples/nlp_vocab_count.cjcl
examples/piml_demo.cjcl
examples/pinn_demo.cjcl
examples/transformer_forward.cjcl
examples/vizor_bar.cjcl
...
11 files found


cjcl drift <a> <b> — Mathematical and data diff engine
Compares two files cell-by-cell with optional numeric tolerance. Auto-detects mode from file extension, or specify explicitly.
Modes: --text | --numeric | --csv
Flags: -e, --tolerance <N> | --precision <N> | --max-diffs <N> | -d, --delimiter <c> | --plain / --json / --color
$ cjcl drift a.csv b.csv --csv

┌──────────────────┬──────────┐
│ Metric           │ Value    │
├──────────────────┼──────────┤
│ Rows (A)         │ 3        │
│ Rows (B)         │ 3        │
│ Cell differences │ 1        │
│ NaN divergences  │ 0        │
│ Max deviation    │ 1.000000 │
│ Frobenius norm   │ 1.000000 │
└──────────────────┴──────────┘

First differences:
  [3,3]: "6" → "7"


cjcl forge <action> — Content-addressable pipeline runner
Runs a program, hashes the output, and caches the result. Re-running with the same source skips execution.
Actions: run <file.cjcl> | verify <file.cjcl> <hash> | list | show <hash-prefix> | clean
Flags: --seed <N> | --cache-dir <path> | -v, --verbose | --plain / --json / --color
$ cjcl forge run fibonacci.cjcl

55
┌──────────┬──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ Property │ Value                                                            │
├──────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ Source   │ fibonacci.cjcl                                                    │
│ Seed     │ 42                                                               │
│ SHA-256  │ 02d20bbd7e394ad5999a4cebabac9619732c343a4cac99470c03e23ba2bdc2bc │
│ Artifact │ .cjcl-forge/02d20bbd...bdc2bc.artifact                            │
└──────────┴──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
forged



Phase 2 CLI Suite — Analysis & Diagnostics

cjcl inspect <file> — Deep file inspection
Analyzes .cjcl, .csv, .snap, or generic files. For CJC-Lang source, reports parse results, function signatures, and structural statistics.
Flags: --no-stats | --no-hash | --preview <N> | --plain / --json / --color
$ cjcl inspect fibonacci.cjcl

┌──────────────┬────────────────┐
│ Property     │ Value          │
├──────────────┼────────────────┤
│ File         │ fibonacci.cjcl  │
│ Type         │ cjcl source    │
│ Size         │ 192 B          │
│ Lines        │ 10             │
│ Parse errors │ 0              │
│ Functions    │ 2              │
│ Structs      │ 0              │
│ Let bindings │ 0              │
└──────────────┴────────────────┘

Functions:
  fn fibonacci(n: i64)
  fn main()


cjcl schema <file.csv> — CSV/TSV schema inference
Infers column types, null counts, unique values, and numeric ranges from CSV data.
Flags: -d, --delimiter <c> | --tsv | --no-header | --no-uniques | --sample <N> | --plain / --json / --color
$ cjcl schema data.csv

Schema for `data.csv` (3 rows sampled):

┌────────┬──────┬───────┬───────┬────────┬────────┬────────┐
│ Column │ Type │ Nulls │ Total │ Unique │ Min    │ Max    │
├────────┼──────┼───────┼───────┼────────┼────────┼────────┤
│ a      │ int  │ 0     │ 3     │ 3      │ 1.0000 │ 7.0000 │
│ b      │ int  │ 0     │ 3     │ 3      │ 2.0000 │ 8.0000 │
│ c      │ int  │ 0     │ 3     │ 3      │ 3.0000 │ 9.0000 │
└────────┴──────┴───────┴───────┴────────┴────────┴────────┘


cjcl trace <file.cjcl> — Deterministic execution tracing
Traces parse time, execution time, AST structure, and runtime behavior.
Flags: --seed <N> | --ast | --no-output | -v, --verbose | --plain / --json / --color
$ cjcl trace fibonacci.cjcl

[trace] Tracing `fibonacci.cjcl`...

┌────────────┬──────────┐
│ Phase      │ Detail   │
├────────────┼──────────┤
│ Parse time │ 0.120 ms │
│ Exec time  │ 1.022 ms │
│ Seed       │ 42       │
│ Status     │ OK       │
└────────────┴──────────┘

┌──────────────┬───────┐
│ AST Element  │ Count │
├──────────────┼───────┤
│ Functions    │ 2     │
│ Structs      │ 0     │
│ Let bindings │ 0     │
│ Statements   │ 0     │
│ Decorators   │ 0     │
└──────────────┴───────┘

┌────────────────┬───────┐
│ Runtime        │ Value │
├────────────────┼───────┤
│ Output lines   │ 1     │
│ GC collections │ 0     │
└────────────────┴───────┘


cjcl mem <file.cjcl> — Runtime memory visibility
Profiles GC collections, heap object counts, output size, and execution time. Supports NoGC pre-verification and executor selection.
Flags: --seed <N> | -n, --runs <N> | -v, --verbose | --nogc | --mir | --eval | --plain / --json / --color
$ cjcl mem fibonacci.cjcl

[mem] Memory profile for `fibonacci.cjcl` (1 run, seed=42)

┌───────────────────────┬──────────┐
│ Metric                │ Value    │
├───────────────────────┼──────────┤
│ GC collections (avg)  │ 0.00     │
│ GC stable across runs │ true     │
│ GC heap objects (max) │ 0        │
│ Output lines          │ 1        │
│ Output bytes          │ 2        │
│ Avg exec time         │ 755.0 us │
└───────────────────────┴──────────┘


cjcl bench <file.cjcl> — Performance benchmarking
Runs warmup iterations, then measured iterations with statistical analysis. Reports mean, median, min, max, standard deviation, coefficient of variation, P95, and throughput.
Flags: --seed <N> | -n, --runs <N> | --warmup <N> | -v, --verbose | --nogc | --mir | --eval | --plain / --json / --color
$ cjcl bench fibonacci.cjcl

[bench] Benchmarking `fibonacci.cjcl` (1 warmup + 10 measured runs, seed=42)

┌──────────────┬─────────────────┐
│ Metric       │ Value           │
├──────────────┼─────────────────┤
│ Parse time   │ 129.0 us        │
│ Mean         │ 647.8 us        │
│ Median       │ 628.5 us        │
│ Min          │ 438.0 us        │
│ Max          │ 808.0 us        │
│ Std dev      │ 111.6 us        │
│ CV           │ 17.2%           │
│ P95          │ 808.0 us        │
│ Throughput   │ 1543.7 runs/sec │
└──────────────┴─────────────────┘


cjcl pack <file.cjcl> — Reproducible packaging
Bundles source files and their referenced artifacts (.snap, .csv, .tsv, .json) into a content-addressed package with manifest hashes.
Flags: -o, --output <dir> | --include <file> (repeatable) | --dry-run | --plain / --json / --color
$ cjcl pack fibonacci.cjcl --dry-run

Dry run — would create `fibonacci.pack`:

┌───────────────┬───────┬──────────────────┐
│ File          │ Size  │ Hash             │
├───────────────┼───────┼──────────────────┤
│ fibonacci.cjcl │ 192 B │ 3402c8cc3d398ba2 │
└───────────────┴───────┴──────────────────┘
Total: 1 files, 192 B
Manifest hash: fce3865dd0fb4816


cjcl doctor [path] — Project diagnostics and health checks
Scans a directory for CJC source files, snap files, and CSV data. Reports parse errors, type issues, corrupt snap files, ragged CSV rows, nondeterministic patterns, and large files.
Flags: -v, --verbose | --fix | --strict (exit non-zero on warnings, useful for CI) | --plain / --json / --color
$ cjcl doctor examples/

[doctor] Diagnosing `examples/`...

┌──────────────────┬───────┐
│ Category         │ Count │
├──────────────────┼───────┤
│ CJC source files │ 11    │
│ Snap files       │ 0     │
│ CSV/TSV files    │ 0     │
│ Total files      │ 17    │
└──────────────────┴───────┘

  [WARN] examples/etl_csv_parse.cjcl — 3 type issue(s)
  [WARN] examples/transformer_forward.cjcl — 50 type issue(s)
  ...

┌──────────┬───────┐
│ Severity │ Count │
├──────────┼───────┤
│ Warnings │ 9     │
└──────────┴───────┘

OK (with warnings)



Phase 3 CLI Suite — Compiler Visibility & Verification

cjcl emit <file.cjcl> — Dump intermediate representations
Dumps the compiler’s internal representation at any pipeline stage: AST (parsed source), HIR (desugared form), or MIR (register-machine IR). Use --opt to see the MIR after constant folding and dead code elimination, or --diff to compare both side by side.
Flags: -s, --stage <S> (ast, hir, mir) | --opt | --diff | --plain / --json / --color
$ cjcl emit fibonacci.cjcl --stage ast

=== fibonacci.cjcl (AST) ===

fn fibonacci(n: i64) -> i64 {
    if (n <= 1) {
        return n;
    }
    return (fibonacci((n - 1)) + fibonacci((n - 2)));
}
fn main() -> i64 {
    let result = fibonacci(10);
    print(result);
    result
}
$ cjcl emit fibonacci.cjcl --stage mir

=== fibonacci.cjcl (MIR) ===

fn fibonacci(n: i64) -> i64:
  if (n <= 1):
    return n
  return (fibonacci((n - 1)) + fibonacci((n - 2)))

fn main() -> void:
  print(fibonacci(10))


cjcl explain <file.cjcl> — Show desugared/lowered forms
Lowers the source to HIR and displays function signatures, struct definitions, NoGC annotations, and decorators. Use --verbose to see the full HIR body for each function.
Flags: -v, --verbose | --plain / --json / --color
$ cjcl explain fibonacci.cjcl

[explain] Explaining `fibonacci.cjcl`...

fn fibonacci(n: i64)
  body: 2 statements

fn main()
  body: 2 statements + tail expression

┌────────────────┬───────┐
│ Element        │ Count │
├────────────────┼───────┤
│ Functions      │ 2     │
│ Structs        │ 0     │
│ NoGC functions │ 0     │
│ Total items    │ 2     │
└────────────────┴───────┘


cjcl gc <file.cjcl> — GC analysis & allocation timeline
Dedicated garbage collection analysis. Runs the program multiple times and reports GC pause count, peak/average heap objects, allocation timeline, and GC stability across runs.
Flags: --seed <N> | -n, --runs <N> | -v, --verbose | --plain / --json / --color
$ cjcl gc fibonacci.cjcl

[gc] GC analysis for `fibonacci.cjcl` (3 runs, seed=42)

┌────────────────────────┬─────────────────────┐
│ Metric                 │ Value               │
├────────────────────────┼─────────────────────┤
│ GC pause count (total) │ 0                   │
│ GC collections (avg)   │ 0.00                │
│ GC collections (min)   │ 0                   │
│ GC collections (max)   │ 0                   │
│ GC stable across runs  │ yes                 │
│ Peak heap objects      │ 0                   │
│ Avg heap objects       │ 0.0                 │
│ COW copies             │ 0 (not yet tracked) │
│ Avg exec time          │ 825.3 us            │
└────────────────────────┴─────────────────────┘

Allocation timeline:
┌─────┬────┬───────────┬──────────────┬──────────────┬───────────┐
│ Run │ GC │ Heap Objs │ Output Lines │ Output Bytes │ Time (us) │
├─────┼────┼───────────┼──────────────┼──────────────┼───────────┤
│ 1   │ 0  │ 0         │ 1            │ 2            │ 816       │
│ 2   │ 0  │ 0         │ 1            │ 2            │ 1006      │
│ 3   │ 0  │ 0         │ 1            │ 2            │ 394       │
└─────┴────┴───────────┴──────────────┴──────────────┴───────────┘

STABLE — GC behavior is deterministic across 3 runs.


cjc nogc <file.cjcl> — NoGC static verification
Verifies that all nogc functions in the program are free of GC allocations. Lists every function with its annotation status and per-function PASS/FAIL. Exit code 1 if any violations are found.
Flags: -v, --verbose | --plain / --json / --color
$ cjcl nogc fibonacci.cjcl

[nogc] NoGC verification for `fibonacci.cjcl`

┌───────────┬─────────┬────────┐
│ Function  │ #[nogc] │ Status │
├───────────┼─────────┼────────┤
│ fibonacci │ no      │ PASS   │
│ main      │ no      │ PASS   │
└───────────┴─────────┴────────┘

Verdict: PASS (2 functions, 0 #[nogc])


cjc audit <file.cjcl> — Numerical hygiene analysis
Static analysis of CJC-Lang source for floating-point anti-patterns. Catches common numerical issues before they become silent precision bugs.
Patterns detected: naive summation in loops (suggests kahan_sum() or binned_sum()), float equality comparison (suggests approx_eq), division without zero-guard, catastrophic cancellation.
Flags: -v, --verbose | --plain / --json / --color
$ cjcl audit naive_sum.cjcl

[audit] Auditing `naive_sum.cjcl`...

  [WARN] line 1: naive summation: `total = total + ...` in loop
         suggestion: use `kahan_sum()` or `binned_sum()`
  [WARN] line 1: naive summation: `i = i + ...` in loop
         suggestion: use `kahan_sum()` or `binned_sum()`

┌────────────────┬───────┐
│ Metric         │ Count │
├────────────────┼───────┤
│ Warnings       │ 4     │
│ Info           │ 0     │
│ Total findings │ 4     │
└────────────────┴───────┘
A clean program reports no findings:
$ cjcl audit fibonacci.cjcl

[audit] Auditing `fibonacci.cjcl`...

CLEAN — no numerical hygiene issues found


cjc precision <file.cjcl> — Precision analysis
Runs the program, captures all numeric output, and compares each f64 value against its f32-truncated approximation. Reports per-value relative error and an overall stability verdict.
Flags: --seed <N> | --plain / --json / --color
$ cjcl precision fibonacci.cjcl

[precision] Precision analysis for `fibonacci.cjcl` (seed=42)

┌──────┬─────────────────┬─────────────────┬────────────┐
│ Line │ f64             │ f32             │ Rel. error │
├──────┼─────────────────┼─────────────────┼────────────┤
│ 1    │ 55.000000000000 │ 55.000000000000 │ 0.00e+00   │
└──────┴─────────────────┴─────────────────┴────────────┘

Max relative error: 0.00e+00
Verdict: STABLE (within f32 epsilon bounds)


cjc lock <file.cjcl> — Deterministic lockfile generator & verifier
Generates a lockfile recording source SHA-256, seed, executor version, platform, and expected output SHA-256. Use --verify to check that the current run matches the lockfile. Enables cross-platform and cross-version reproducibility verification.
Flags: --seed <N> | --verify | --plain / --json / --color
$ cjcl lock fibonacci.cjcl

[lock] Generated lockfile: fibonacci.cjcl.lock
┌───────────────┬──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ Field         │ Value                                                            │
├───────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ source_sha256 │ 3402c8cc3d398ba214ee1111095e3568f0617857785ed8e1f9ec74e5eea1adda │
│ seed          │ 42                                                               │
│ executor      │ eval v0.1.0                                                      │
│ platform      │ windows-x86_64                                                   │
│ output_sha256 │ 02d20bbd7e394ad5999a4cebabac9619732c343a4cac99470c03e23ba2bdc2bc │
└───────────────┴──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

title: Syntax Guide for CJC Version 0.1.1
text:
Part 10 — Design Decisions and Trade-offs

Why No HashMap?
HashMap uses random iteration order in Rust (and most languages). This means any code that iterates over a hash map produces non-deterministic results. CJC-Lang uses BTreeMap and BTreeSet exclusively, trading some average-case performance for guaranteed deterministic iteration.


Why No FMA?
Fused multiply-add (FMA) computes a * b + c in a single instruction with one rounding step instead of two. This is faster and more accurate — but it produces different results depending on whether the hardware has FMA support. CJC-Lang uses mul_fixed (separate multiply and add) to guarantee identical results across all platforms.


Why Two Execution Engines?
The AST interpreter (cjc-eval) is simpler and easier to debug. The MIR executor (cjc-mir-exec) is faster and supports optimization. Having both means:

New features can be prototyped in the interpreter first
Parity tests catch subtle lowering bugs
Users can choose the right trade-off for their workload



Why Vec-Based Pauli Terms?
Quantum Hamiltonians are sums of Pauli terms. A natural representation is a hash map from Pauli string to coefficient. CJC-Lang uses Vec<PauliTerm> instead, because:

Iteration order is deterministic
No hashing overhead for small Hamiltonians
Summation order is fixed, so Kahan compensation works reliably



Why Sign-Stabilized SVD?
SVD is ambiguous in the signs of singular vectors. Two runs of the same SVD can produce vectors that differ by a sign flip. CJC-Lang’s MPS canonical form uses a sign convention (largest-magnitude element is positive) to eliminate this ambiguity. This is critical for deterministic tensor network simulations.

title: Syntax Guide for CJC Version 0.1.1
text:
What’s Next
CJC-Lang v0.1.1 lays the groundwork for quantum-classical hybrid algorithms, noise-aware quantum simulation, and tensor network methods. Upcoming work includes:

Multi-file module system — programs that span multiple files with deterministic resolution
Default parameters and variadic functions — fn solve(x: f64, tol: f64 = 1e-6)
Decorator syntax — @log @timed fn train(x: Tensor)
Sparse eigensolvers — Lanczos and Arnoldi with deterministic iteration
MIR autodiff integration — gradients flowing through the MIR optimization pipeline

The language is growing, but the invariants remain fixed: no hidden allocations, no non-deterministic operations, no silent precision loss. Same seed, same bits, always.







CJC-Lang is open source and under active development. The codebase spans 21 Rust crates with zero external runtime dependencies. If deterministic numerical computing interests you, check out the introductory post or the v0.1.0 release notes.

title: Introducing CJC-Lang: Why I’m Building a Deterministic Scientific Computing Language
text:
I didn’t set out to build a programming language. I set out to solve a problem.
I spent years working as a lab engineer running experiments on audio devices — collecting data, cleaning it, troubleshooting when results didn’t look right. Was the anomaly real, or was it an environment issue? A script issue? A sensor calibration drift? I learned firsthand how messy data — or messy handling of data — introduces problems that cascade through every stage of analysis. A mistake in the initial data cleaning phase doesn’t just cost time. It costs weeks of downstream work, delays shipping, and erodes confidence in the results.
That experience stuck with me as I moved into data science. In most scientific computing environments, running the same program twice can produce slightly different results. Floating-point reduction order changes between runs. Hash maps iterate in random order. Thread scheduling affects accumulation. RNG state leaks across boundaries. These aren’t bugs — they’re properties of systems that weren’t designed with determinism as a constraint.
I wanted a system where the same program with the same seed produces bit-identical output every time, on every platform. Not approximately the same — exactly the same.
That constraint turned out to touch everything: memory layout, collection types, floating-point arithmetic, random number generation, parallelism. The deeper I went, the clearer it became that bolting determinism onto an existing language would mean fighting the language at every turn. So I built one where determinism is structural.

title: Introducing CJC-Lang: Why I’m Building a Deterministic Scientific Computing Language
text:
What Is CJC-Lang?
CJC-Lang — Computation Jacobian Core — is a programming language I’m building from scratch for deterministic scientific computing. It’s written in Rust, organized as a 20-crate workspace, with nearly all functionality implemented from scratch using only the Rust standard library.






CJC-Lang is currently in beta. The core compiler pipeline and runtime are functional and backed by 5,300+ passing tests, but the language is still under active development and is not production-ready. I’m sharing it now because the ideas behind it are worth discussing even before everything is polished.





20Rust crates


6.4 MBsingle binary


~300built-in functions


5,300+tests passing


The language covers the full compilation and execution pipeline: a lexer, recursive descent parser, type checker, two intermediate representations (HIR and MIR), an optimizer, two execution engines, and a runtime with ~300 built-in functions for math, statistics, linear algebra, machine learning, signal processing, and data manipulation.

title: Introducing CJC-Lang: Why I’m Building a Deterministic Scientific Computing Language
text:
Core Ideas

Determinism as Architecture
CJC-Lang enforces determinism at every layer:

Ordered collections everywhere. BTreeMap and BTreeSet instead of hash-based containers. Iteration order is always defined.
Stable floating-point reductions. All summations use Kahan or binned accumulators — order-invariant superaccumulators that produce identical results regardless of evaluation order.
No FMA in SIMD kernels. Fused multiply-add produces different rounding than separate multiply and add. CJC-Lang avoids FMA to keep results identical across CPU architectures.
Seeded, explicit RNG. The random number generator is SplitMix64 with explicit seed threading. No global state, no entropy sources.
Deterministic parallelism. Parallel operations are structured so that results don’t depend on thread count or scheduling.

This isn’t a mode you opt into. It’s how the language works. The determinism guarantee is verified in the test suite — for example, the SciML tests train a physics-informed model twice with the same seed and assert that every parameter is bit-identical:
// From tests/sciml_determinism.rs
let r1 = piml_heat_1d_train(6, 20, 30, 0.01, 1000, 1e-3, 1.0, 10.0, 42);
let r2 = piml_heat_1d_train(6, 20, 30, 0.01, 1000, 1e-3, 1.0, 10.0, 42);
for (i, (&a, &b)) in r1.final_params.iter()
    .zip(r2.final_params.iter()).enumerate()
{
    assert_eq!(a.to_bits(), b.to_bits(),
        "Param {} not bit-identical", i);
}
Not approximately equal. Bit-identical. to_bits() compares the raw IEEE 754 representation.


Nearly Zero External Dependencies
The entire system compiles to a 6.4 MB single binary. That one file contains everything: lexer, parser, type checker, two execution engines, optimizer, ~300 builtins, tensor library, linear algebra kernels, regex engine, AD engine, data manipulation DSL, serialization, visualization, and 15+ CLI subcommands.1
This is a deliberate choice. External dependencies introduce version churn, platform variance, and complexity that’s hard to audit. For a system that promises bit-identical results, every component needs to be under direct control.


Dual Execution Engines
CJC-Lang has two execution paths:

AST interpreter (cjc-eval): walks the syntax tree directly. Simple, easy to debug, good for prototyping.
MIR executor (cjc-mir-exec): lowers through HIR → MIR, applies optimization passes (constant folding, dead code elimination), and runs on a register machine.

Both engines must produce identical results for every program. Parity tests enforce this — if the engines disagree, that’s a bug, and the test suite catches it.


NoGC Verification
CJC-Lang includes a static verifier that can prove code paths are free of GC allocations. The NoGC verifier performs transitive call-graph analysis to ensure that annotated functions — and everything they call — operate within reference-counted, copy-on-write memory without triggering GC. This matters for latency-sensitive workloads where GC pauses are unacceptable.

title: Introducing CJC-Lang: Why I’m Building a Deterministic Scientific Computing Language
Introducing CJC-Lang: Why I’m Building a Deterministic Scientific Computing Language
text:
Beyond Neural Networks
CJC-Lang isn’t just a machine learning language. The numerical infrastructure supports a broader range of scientific computing — and this is where the determinism guarantee pays the biggest dividends. When you’re debugging a failed ODE integration or a clustering result that doesn’t match your colleague’s run, knowing the computation is reproducible eliminates an entire class of “was it the code or was it the environment?” questions.







Domain
Key Capabilities




ODE/PDE Solvers
Euler, RK4, adaptive RK45, 1D Laplacian, diffusion stepping, adjoint sensitivity


Optimization
Gradient descent, BFGS, L-BFGS, Nelder-Mead, bisection, Brent, Newton, secant


Regression
OLS, weighted least squares, logistic regression, polynomial fitting


Clustering
K-means (k-means++ init), DBSCAN, agglomerative (4 linkage methods), PCA


Time Series
ACF, PACF, EWMA, seasonal decomposition, stationarity tests (ADF, KPSS, Phillips-Perron)


Interpolation
Cubic natural splines, linear, nearest-neighbor, Horner polynomial evaluation




Adaptive RK45 integration with step size control

let result = ode_solve_rk45(
    |t: f64, y: f64| -0.5 * y,   // dy/dt = -0.5y (exponential decay)
    0.0,                           // t_start
    10.0,                          // t_end
    1.0,                           // y_initial
    1e-6                           // tolerance
);
print("Final y:", result);         // ~0.00674 (matches exp(-5))

title: Introducing CJC-Lang: Why I’m Building a Deterministic Scientific Computing Language
section: What’s Native — and Why That Matters
text:
What’s Native — and Why That Matters
Everything mentioned above runs inside a single binary with nearly zero external dependencies. Every component is under direct control, which means determinism guarantees extend through the entire stack — from CSV parsing through model training through serialization.








Component
What It Does
Why Native Matters




Regex (cjc-regex)
Thompson NFA engine — O(n) linear time, no backtracking
Eliminates ReDoS vulnerabilities and supply chain risk


RNG (SplitMix64)
Seeded, explicit state, fork() for parallel streams
Same seed = same output on every platform


Visualization (cjc-vizor)
Grammar-of-graphics with SVG/BMP output
No subprocess, no JS engine, no network calls


DataFrame (cjc-data)
73+ tidyverse-style ops, lazy eval, typed columns
Grouping and aggregation inherit determinism automatically


Serialization (cjc-snap)
Canonical binary format with SHA-256 content hashing
Checkpoints are exact; snap_hash() enables verification across runs


Accumulators (cjc-repro)
Kahan compensated and binned superaccumulators
Order-invariant reductions — the mechanism behind bit-identical floating-point results



No pip install. No npm install. No version conflicts. Just the binary, the source file, and the seed.

title: Introducing CJC-Lang: Why I’m Building a Deterministic Scientific Computing Language
text:
The CLI Toolchain
CJC-Lang ships with 15+ CLI subcommands:
cjcl run program.cjcl              # execute a program
cjcl run program.cjcl --seed 42    # explicit RNG seed
cjcl run program.cjcl --mir-opt    # MIR-optimized execution
cjcl check program.cjcl            # type-check without running
cjcl repl                         # interactive REPL
cjcl trace program.cjcl            # execution tracing
cjcl bench program.cjcl            # benchmarking
cjcl doctor                       # health/validation checks
Additional commands include inspect, schema, flow (control flow graphs), drift (determinism drift detection), mem (memory profiling), pack (binary serialization), and forge (code generation).

title: Introducing CJC-Lang: Why I’m Building a Deterministic Scientific Computing Language
section: Why CJC-Lang Is Still in Beta
text:
Why CJC-Lang Is Still in Beta
The core is solid: the compiler pipeline works end-to-end, both execution engines agree, the test suite is extensive, and the runtime covers a wide surface area. But several areas need more work:

Type inference is limited — most types require explicit annotation
Generics are parsed but not fully enforced at the type level
The module system exists but needs refinement for multi-file projects
REPL lacks readline support
Optimizer has room for more passes beyond constant folding and DCE
Documentation is sparse outside of test coverage

I’m not rushing to call this production-ready. The point is to build something correct first, then make it convenient.

title: Introducing CJC-Lang: Why I’m Building a Deterministic Scientific Computing Language
text:
What Comes Next
The near-term roadmap includes:

If-expressions: converting if from a statement to an expression (let x = if cond { a } else { b })
Default parameters and variadic functions: making function signatures more flexible
MIR-integrated autodiff: ensuring gradient computation works through the optimized execution path
Module system improvements: reliable multi-file program support
Sparse linear algebra: eigenvalue solvers (Lanczos, Arnoldi) with deterministic iteration

title: Introducing CJC-Lang: Why I’m Building a Deterministic Scientific Computing Language
text:
Why Share This Now?
CJC-Lang isn’t finished, but the ideas behind it are clear enough to discuss. Deterministic scientific computing is a real problem — I’ve seen it firsthand, from the lab bench to the data pipeline. Building the entire stack from scratch is an unusual approach, but the results so far suggest it’s viable.
If you’re interested in compiler design, numerical methods, reproducible computing, or just the experience of building a language from the ground up, I’ll be writing more here as the project develops.
Next up: I’ll walk through the neural network architectures I’ve built and verified in CJC-Lang — REINFORCE agents, physics-informed networks, CNNs, and attention mechanisms, all running deterministically without any ML framework.






Follow the project



CJC-Lang is open source and under active development. The project is in beta — functional and tested, but not yet production-ready. Follow @adamezzat for updates as new features land.

The only external dependencies are in the optional language server (cjc-analyzer) and the rayon crate for parallel tensor operations. The core 18 crates have zero external dependencies.↩︎

title: Neural Networks in CJC-Lang: From MLPs to Attention, Without a Framework
text:
🕑 ~15 min read
In the previous post, I introduced CJC-Lang and the design decisions behind its determinism guarantees. This post goes deeper into one specific question: can you build real neural network architectures in a language with no ML framework?
The answer is yes — and the determinism makes verification straightforward. Every architecture below has been implemented in pure CJC-Lang, run through both execution engines, and verified with assertions. Same seed, same output, every time.

title: Neural Networks in CJC-Lang: From MLPs to Attention, Without a Framework
Neural Networks in CJC-Lang: From MLPs to Attention, Without a Framework
Neural Networks in CJC-Lang: From MLPs to Attention, Without a Framework
text:
Physics-Informed Neural Network (PINN)
CJC-Lang’s AD engine supports PINNs — neural networks that learn to satisfy differential equations, not just fit data. This is where determinism isn’t just convenient — it’s scientifically necessary.




flowchart TB
    X["Position x"] --> N["MLP [1 → 32 → 32 → 1]\ntanh activation"] --> U["Prediction u_hat"]
    U --> DL["Data Loss\nMSE"]
    U --> PL["Physics Loss\nu_xx + u ≈ 0"]
    U --> BL["Boundary Loss\nu(0)=0, u(pi)=0"]
    DL --> T["Total Loss"]
    PL --> T
    BL --> T
    T -.->|"Adam optimizer"| N


PINN for the harmonic oscillator. The composite loss combines data fitting, PDE residual, and boundary conditions.



The harmonic oscillator example uses a [1, 32, 32, 1] MLP with tanh activation trained to solve u_xx + u = 0:

Data loss: MSE against observed points (optionally noisy)
Physics loss: the PDE residual u_xx + u computed via finite differences through the network, penalized toward zero
Boundary loss: u(0) and u(π) enforced to match known boundary conditions
Optimizer: Adam with learning rate scheduling

The exact solution is sin(x), and the trained network converges to it with verified L2 error below tolerance.
When you’re claiming a network satisfies a PDE, you need to reproduce the training run exactly to verify the claim. CJC-Lang’s deterministic AD engine, seeded RNG, and Kahan-summed loss computations mean the verification is airtight. The test suite trains the same PINN twice with seed 42 and asserts that every parameter is bit-identical using to_bits() comparison on the raw IEEE 754 representation. No other ML framework provides this guarantee without significant configuration overhead.

title: Neural Networks in CJC-Lang: From MLPs to Attention, Without a Framework
text:
Physics-Informed ML (PIML)
A simpler but equally rigorous approach: polynomial regression with a physics residual penalty, applied to the 1D heat equation u_xx = f(x).
The model learns polynomial coefficients that simultaneously fit noisy observations and satisfy the PDE. Boundary conditions are enforced through weighted penalty terms. The exact solution is sin(πx), and the optimized polynomial converges to it.

title: Neural Networks in CJC-Lang: From MLPs to Attention, Without a Framework
text:
Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN)
CJC-Lang’s tensor runtime includes native 1D and 2D convolution, maxpooling, and the full pipeline for building convolutional architectures.




flowchart LR
    I["Input\n[4,3,28,28]"] --> C["Conv2D\n32 filters 3x3"] --> R["ReLU"] --> BN["BatchNorm"] --> MP["MaxPool2D\n2x2"] --> O["Output\n[4,32,13,13]"]


CNN pipeline in CJC-Lang. Native conv2d, ReLU, batch normalization, and maxpooling — all deterministic.



// 2D convolution: batch of images through learned filters
let input = Tensor.randn([4, 3, 28, 28]);   // batch=4, channels=3, 28x28
let filters = Tensor.randn([32, 3, 3, 3]);  // 32 output channels, 3x3 kernel
let bias = Tensor.zeros([32]);
let conv_out = input.conv2d(filters, bias, 1);   // -> [4, 32, 26, 26]

// Activation + normalization + pooling
let activated = conv_out.relu();
let pooled = activated.maxpool2d(2, 2);          // -> [4, 32, 13, 13]
CJC-Lang also supports 1D convolution with circular padding (conv1d_circular) for signal processing, and the full set of CNN building blocks: batch_norm, dropout_mask, arbitrary strides, and multi-channel operations.

title: Neural Networks in CJC-Lang: From MLPs to Attention, Without a Framework
text:
Attention Mechanism (Transformer Building Block)
Scaled dot-product attention — the core primitive of transformer architectures — is implemented as a native tensor operation.




flowchart LR
    Q["Q [batch, seq, d_k]"] --> MM["Q · K^T"]
    K["K [batch, seq, d_k]"] --> MM
    MM --> SC["÷ sqrt(d_k)"] --> SM["Softmax"] --> MV["× V"]
    V["V [batch, seq, d_k]"] --> MV
    MV --> O["Output\n[batch, seq, d_k]"]


Scaled dot-product attention — softmax(QK^T / sqrt(d_k)) * V — as a single native CJC-Lang operation.



// Scaled dot-product attention: softmax(Q @ K^T / sqrt(d_k)) @ V
let Q = Tensor.randn([2, 8, 64]);   // [batch, seq_len, d_k]
let K = Tensor.randn([2, 8, 64]);
let V = Tensor.randn([2, 8, 64]);
let output = attention(Q, K, V);      // -> [2, 8, 64]
This computes softmax(QK^T / sqrt(d_k)) * V with batch matrix multiplication and deterministic softmax. It’s the core primitive you’d need to build transformer-style architectures.

title: Neural Networks in CJC-Lang: From MLPs to Attention, Without a Framework
text:
Deep Network Stress Tests
Beyond the architecture-specific examples above, CJC-Lang includes benchmark programs that push forward passes to realistic scale:










Benchmark
Depth
Width
Batch
Verified




bench_nn_deep
50 layers
64
4
Bit-identical via snap_hash


bench_nn_wide
10 layers
256

Bit-identical via snap_hash



Both benchmarks insert snap_hash() checkpoints every few layers, producing cryptographic hashes of intermediate activations. Running twice with --seed 42 produces identical hashes at every checkpoint — proving that determinism holds through deep chains of matmul, bias, ReLU, and normalization.

title: Neural Networks in CJC-Lang: From MLPs to Attention, Without a Framework
text:
What This Proves
These aren’t theoretical capabilities. Each architecture has been implemented, run through both execution engines, and verified with assertions:

The REINFORCE agent plays chess (69 tests verify the full pipeline)
The PINN converges to the analytical solution of a differential equation
The CNN processes real 4D tensor shapes with convolution, pooling, and batch normalization
The attention mechanism handles batched multi-sequence inputs
The deep network benchmarks verify hash-level determinism through 50 sequential layers

All of them produce identical results across runs. In CJC-Lang, that’s not an opt-in mode — it’s how computation works.

If you haven’t read the introduction to CJC-Lang and the design decisions behind the language, start with Introducing CJC-Lang.
Next up: I’ll write about what it took to build a dual-engine compiler pipeline in Rust — and the design decisions that made parity testing between an AST interpreter and a register machine actually tractable.
